note: Collected via WebSearch, 2026-07-06. Numbers as reported by secondary summaries of the cited sources; not independently re-derived from raw competition/study data.

m6_forecasting_competition.source: Spiliotis et al., 'The M6 forecasting competition: Bridging the gap between forecasting and investment decisions' (arXiv:2310.13357 / ResearchGate)
m6_forecasting_competition.finding: Of 163 teams on the global leaderboard, only 38 (23.3%) produced more accurate forecasts than the naive benchmark; 47 (28.8%) built better-performing portfolios; only 11 (6.7%) beat the benchmark on both accuracy and investment return; only 3 teams beat the benchmark in all 12 competition months.
m6_forecasting_competition.relevance: Large, well-resourced, professionally-run forecasting competition on real equities; even the best specialist teams mostly failed to beat a naive benchmark, and consistent monthly outperformance was almost nonexistent. Contextualizes why ~50% direction accuracy at scale is the expected regime, not a sign of a broken system.

llm_forecast_vs_analysts.source: 'The Promise and Peril of Generative AI: Evidence from GPT as Sell-Side Analysts' (arXiv:2412.01069)
llm_forecast_vs_analysts.finding: Inferring earnings direction from GPT's numerical forecasts yielded 49.3% accuracy; explicit directional prompting yielded 47.7% accuracy; human sell-side analysts achieved 71.1% on the same task.
llm_forecast_vs_analysts.relevance: Directly comparable to tokenstree.es's ensemble at 0.472-0.510 hit rate: an LLM/model-based directional call landing at ~48-49%, indistinguishable from or below coin-flip, is a documented outcome in the literature for financial direction prediction, not unique to this system.

other_studies_noted.finding: Directional accuracy of 45.0%, statistically indistinguishable from 50%, reported in a stock direction prediction study
other_studies_noted.source: Cited in secondary aggregation of stock-direction-prediction literature (exact primary source not isolated in this search pass)

caveats: A broader web query on 'day trader win rate' surfaced mostly low-quality SEO/marketing content (prop-firm blogs, trading-education sites) rather than peer-reviewed studies; those numbers (e.g. '1-4% of day traders profitable') are NOT included as literature evidence here because they are not verifiable academic sources. Only the M6 competition paper and the GPT-sell-side-analyst paper are treated as citable literature.

The 'high-performance' deep-learning papers surfaced in search (66-95% directional accuracy) are typically small-sample, single-asset, or in-sample/backtested results without walk-forward validation disclosed in the abstract; they are not used as counter-evidence, just noted as existing in the literature landscape.
